title: Corners with Polynomial Side Length
speaker: Noah Kravitz
abstract:
We prove ''reasonable'' quantitative bounds for sets in ℤ2
 avoiding the polynomial corner configuration (x,y),(x+P(z),y),(x,y+P(z)), where P is any fixed integer-coefficient polynomial with an integer root of multiplicity 1. This simultaneously generalizes a result of Shkredov about corner-free sets and a recent result of Peluse, Sah, and Sawhney about sets without 3-term arithmetic progressions of common difference z2−1. Two ingredients in our proof are a general quantitative concatenation result for multidimensional polynomial progressions and a new degree-lowering argument for box norms.  Joint work with Borys Kuca and James Leng.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Ramsey context and polynomial-corner theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk situates polynomial corners among Roth/Szemerédi, Sárközy, Bergelson--Leibman, and known quantitative results, then states the new bound for dense subsets of [N]^2 avoiding corners whose common side length lies in the image of a polynomial with a simple integer root.

proof_stages.label: Counting operators and naive transference
proof_stages.summary: The number of polynomial corners is encoded by a trilinear counting operator Lambda_p, and the first attempted strategy is to compare it uniformly with an ordinary-corner operator so that Shkredov's quantitative corner theorem would imply the desired result.

proof_stages.label: Archimedean and local corrections
proof_stages.summary: The naive comparison is repaired by adding a smooth decaying weight to model the sparse polynomial image and by applying the W-trick/presetting to remove small-modulus biases, yielding a transfer theorem comparing the W-tricked polynomial operator with the model corner operator.

proof_stages.label: Box-norm inverse reduction for transfer
proof_stages.summary: To prove the transfer theorem, the argument reduces large polynomial or model counts to correlation of f_0 with a separable function g(x)h(y), introduces box norms, proves the elementary inverse theorem for the U^1 x U^1 norm, and formulates the main U^1 x U^1 control statement.

proof_stages.label: PET induction and quantitative concatenation
proof_stages.summary: The first major technical step obtains a high-degree box-norm version of the control theorem by repeated Cauchy--Schwarz/PET induction, then uses quantitative concatenation to convert averages of box norms with polynomially varying directions into a single box norm via nearly equidistributed multilinear expressions.

proof_stages.label: Degree lowering via nilsequence analysis
proof_stages.summary: The second major technical step lowers the high-degree box control to U^1 x U^1 control using dual-difference interchange and stashing, reducing to nilsequence-exponential correlations and then applying quantitative inverse/equidistribution machinery plus the simple-root arithmetic input to force major-arc structure.

examples.name: Three-term arithmetic progressions
examples.note: Roth's configuration x,x+y,x+2y is used as the familiar linear benchmark for density Ramsey results, as a special case of Bergelson--Leibman, and later as the standard configuration controlled by the U^2/Fourier norm.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines three-term progressions and cites Roth/Gowers-control facts, but does not carry out a calculation on that configuration.

examples.name: Even numbers obstruct adjacent pairs
examples.note: The set of even integers shows that one cannot force configurations x,x+1 in a dense set, motivating the zero-constant-term/local-obstruction condition in polynomial Szemerédi-type statements.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses the even numbers as a concrete local obstruction to adjacent pairs, verifying that one cannot guarantee an x,x+1 configuration.

examples.name: The polynomial z^2+z
examples.note: The congruence bias of z^2+z modulo 3 visibly forces the W-trick correction, and the same polynomial later illustrates how complicated the PET output becomes for polynomial corners.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes z^2+z modulo 3 and then runs the W-trick on it, deriving the transformed polynomial wz^2+z and its congruence behavior.

examples.name: Gowers norms as box-norm special cases
examples.note: When the underlying group is finite and all boxes coincide, the abstract box norm becomes the Gowers U^s norm, providing a familiar special case of the norm technology used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: Gowers norms are identified as special cases of box norms and some known facts are stated, but the speaker does not work through that specialization in detail.
